input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin is a 1/10-scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology, featuring lower and upper level chassis layers and autonomy elements, designed for Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) within Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) applications.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides three key services: 1) Data Monitoring via a UI for real-time visualization of vehicle state data, projected driving paths, and detected object tracks, with capabilities to save and generate reports; 2) Computation Offloading where the ACC system (including lead vehicle detection, speed computation based on distance, and actuation commands) is offloaded from on-board ECUs to cloud-based resources; 3) Remote Management allowing operators to enable/disable ACC, set desired speeds, and issue emergency braking commands through the UI. Target users include operators for real-time monitoring and control, and developers for troubleshooting using collected data. This system adheres to the Fully Autonomous Digital Twin with Human-in-the-Loop taxonomy.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 software development kit, ROS2 middleware, Python, C++, and MATLAB/Simulink programming languages.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The system requires 100 ms clock synchronization accuracy for coordination between devices to enable computation offloading, as mandated by hardware limitations. While safety-critical vehicular standards typically require stricter synchronization than 100 ms, the current implementation adheres to the 100 ms threshold due to existing hardware constraints.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system employs a hierarchical architecture with three distinct entities (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, and User), each deployed on separate hardware components. The User Entity includes two subcomponents (3D Visualization and Control Panel), while the Physical Twin Entity deviates from ISO 23247 standards by encompassing inherent system resources (compute, sensors, actuators) and hosting general automotive functions like Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking, rather than being limited to resource-specific functionality as in the standard's OME.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system architecture follows ISO 23247 RA, mapping software components to Functional Entities (FEs) implemented as individual ROS2 packages, each containing one or more ROS2 nodes. The three core entities—Physical Twin, Digital Twin, and User—are organized to handle resource management, data processing, and user interaction respectively, with Physical Twin Entity specifically managing sensor data pre-processing, actuation, and device communication functionality on dedicated hardware.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data from physical sensors (LiDAR, vehicle state, motor, steering servo) is collected by Data Collecting FE and processed by Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking SW component. The Data Translation component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for all streams. Communication between physical and digital entities occurs via Wi-Fi UDP transport layer, while ROS2 handles internal software component communication. Data types include raw point clouds, vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving paths, transmitted in real-time.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands generated by the ACC Controlling FE are translated via the Data Translation component and sent to the Actuation FE, which drives Motor and Steering Servo actuators. Commands are communicated using ROS2 for internal software components and Wi-Fi UDP for inter-entity transmission.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication between software components occurs within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Ego vehicle state data is stored on a device external to the vehicle. No additional details on hosting platforms, computational requirements, scalability, or reliability mechanisms are provided in the document.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process includes defining Digital Twin System requirements (Section IV-A), architectural design (Section IV-B), and implementation of Digital Twin services (Section IV-C) for the Adaptive Cruise Control case study.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Functional Entities transform data into human-interpretable formats, and the User Entity provides interfaces for monitoring data and controlling the Observable Manufacturing Element (OME), enabling decision support through data visualization and system control.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliance with ISO 23247 standard (parts 1-4), where Part 2 defines the reference architecture entities (Observable Manufacturing Element, Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity), and Functional Entities implement tasks such as data transformation using protocols like OPC Unified Architecture for interoperability.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction is highly specific and perfectly matches the description of the physical twin in Section III.B. It accurately captures the scale (1/10), the base vehicle (Traxxas teleoperated), the retrofitting nature (advanced sensor and control technology), the chassis structure (lower and upper layers), and the specific application (ACC within ADAS).

The description is comprehensive and strongly grounded in Section III.A. It correctly identifies the three key services (Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, Remote Management) with specific details such as the UI capabilities, offloading of ACC logic to the cloud/DT, and remote commands. It also accurately lists the target users and the taxonomy classification.

The description accurately lists the key development tools and technologies used in implementing the Digital Twin. This is strongly supported by quotes: 'We use NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 as the Software Development Kit (SDK) for programming the Jetson Orin Nano, and Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) [32] as the middleware to integrate application modules.'; 'Application modules are implemented in Python 3.8 (rapid prototyping), C++ (ROS2 node lifecycle management), and MATLAB/Simulink (for more complex systems, e.g., point cloud clustering and tracking).'

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately captures the 100 ms clock synchronization requirement required for computation offloading and correctly attributes this threshold to hardware limitations rather than stricter safety standards. Quotes: 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms', 'To enable computation offloading, devices must share the same notion of time', 'We require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations'.

The description correctly identifies the three entities deployed on separate hardware, the specific subcomponents of the User Entity, and the deviation from ISO 23247 where the Physical Twin Entity hosts general functions like Point Cloud Clustering instead of just resource-specific OME functionality. Quotes: 'Considering that each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component', 'singular User Interface FE is instantiated in the 3D Visualization and Control Panel components', 'Physical Twin Entity reflects the inherent resources... and can host general automotive functionality, such as Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking'.

The description provides a detailed technical overview of the architecture, correctly mapping software components to ISO 23247 Functional Entities implemented as ROS2 packages. It accurately summarizes the division of responsibilities across the three entities. Quotes: 'We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design phase', 'implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes', 'Data pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the Physical Twin Entity'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', which is correct. The provided source text focuses on the internal architecture of the specific Digital Twin system (Physical, Digital, and User entities) and does not discuss integration with external systems, interoperability with other Digital Twins, or enterprise-level data exchange.

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_3_judge.reasoning: The characteristic is marked as 'Not in Document'. The text discusses the architecture, services (monitoring, offloading, remote control), and requirements of the ACC Digital Twin. It mentions the use of a 3D simulator (related work) and data collection, but does not describe specific computational model types (geometric, behavioral), detailed data sources, or model relationships within the DT constellation. Therefore, the claim is correct.

The characteristic is marked as 'Not in Document'. This is incorrect. The text explicitly identifies physical actuators such as the 'Vedder Electronic Speed Controller' and 'Steering Servo'. It also describes the 'Actuation' component which 'Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands' (Table I) and lists 'Motor' and 'Steering Servo' in Figure 2.

The characteristic is marked as 'Not in Document'. This is incorrect. The text specifies sensors in the physical twin: 'Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR', 'Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera', and implies 'Vehicle State' sensing. Figure 2 also lists 'LiDAR' and 'Vehicle State' as inputs to the system.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', and the source documents do not contain specific information regarding model fidelity requirements, validation and verification methods for the implemented DT, or uncertainty quantification. While the introduction defines DTs generically as 'high-fidelity computational reflections' and the requirements list performance metrics like latency and clock synchronization, it does not describe fidelity/validity processes for the specific system.

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. Raw point cloud data from the LiDAR sensor must be processed by the _Point Cloud_ _Clustering & Tracking_ SW component, which turns the raw point cloud, represented as individual points on a 2D plane,

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly describes the data flow from sensors (LiDAR, vehicle state, motor, steering servo) to the Data Collecting FE and Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking component. It accurately mentions the Data Translation component's role in serialization, alignment, and unit conversion, as well as the communication protocols (ROS2 internally, Wi-Fi UDP externally). The data types listed (point clouds, vehicle state, object tracks, projected paths) are supported by the text. However, it omits specific hardware details (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson, Hokuyo LiDAR) and software components like Report Generation and Data Storage. The extraction is correct and specific but could be expanded with more technical details.

The extraction correctly identifies the control flow from the ACC Controlling FE to the Actuation FE, which drives the Motor and Steering Servo. It accurately states the use of ROS2 for internal communication and Wi-Fi UDP for inter-entity transmission. However, it lacks details on specific command types (e.g., desired speed, emergency braking) and the Actuation FE's conversion of desired speed to motor voltage commands, which are present in the text. The extraction is correct but could be more comprehensive.

The extraction accurately states that internal communication uses ROS2 and inter-entity communication uses Wi-Fi with UDP at the transport layer, directly supported by the text. However, it omits latency requirements (R7), clock synchronization (R8), and network topology details, which are relevant technical details mentioned in the document. The extraction is correct but lacks depth.

The extraction correctly identifies the requirement for external storage (R3). However, it inaccurately states that no computational requirements are provided in the document, whereas the text explicitly details the physical hardware (e.g., 'NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano microcomputer') and discusses cloud offloading. Thus, the score is reduced for the misleading claim about missing information.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task

does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.

block_5_judge.reasoning: The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text mentions the use of DTs during 'design phase' and 'vehicle operation' of the automotive lifecycle, and generally references 'product lifecycle management', but it does not describe the specific lifecycle phases *of the Digital Twin itself*, nor does it detail representation types, transition management, or lifecycle governance as defined in the characteristic description. Therefore, the 'Not in Document' assessment is accurate.

The extraction accurately captures the engineering process described in Section IV. It correctly identifies the sequence of requirements definition, architectural design, and implementation for the specific ACC case study. Supported by the text: 'We now provide a detailed exposition of the case study, starting from the definition of DTS requirements (Sec. IV-A). We then elaborate on the architectural design (Sec. IV-B) and the implementation of DTS services (Sec. IV-C).'

The extraction accurately reflects the roles of Functional Entities and the User Entity as described in the ISO 23247 background section and the case study. It correctly identifies data transformation for human interpretation and interfaces for monitoring and control. Supported by quotes: 'transforming data into a human-interpretable format', 'The User Entity provides interfaces for users to interact with the Digital Twin Entity, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME', and 'user interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate reports'.

The extraction provides a highly specific and detailed summary of the ISO 23247 standard's compliance and structure found in the text. It correctly identifies the parts of the standard, the entities defined in Part 2, and mentions interoperability protocols. Supported by quotes: 'The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview... 2) Reference architecture...', 'The Observable Manufacturing Element... The Data Collection and Device Control Entity... The Digital Twin Entity... Finally, the User Entity', and 'one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture (UA) of IEC 62541 to facilitate the implementation'.

The source text discusses data collection (e.g., 'The DTS shall collect real-time data about the ego vehicle’s state') and storage ('store the ego vehicle state data on a device external to the vehicle'), but it does not contain information regarding data ownership policies, privacy protection, consent management, or data anonymization.
